text: This video highlights the importance of using a circular anal dilator to both diagnose and guide fixation of internal rectal prolapse during rectal prolapse surgery, getting it right first time. Internal rectal prolapse is full thickness interception of the rectum. Whilst it can occur in normal individuals, it has been shown to be morphologically more advanced in symptomatic patients with more high-grade or rectoanal interception present. Patients are usually seen in the pelvic floor clinic and then we'll have specialist investigations. While some centers use conventional x-ray dynamic defecating proctography, we use MRI, which provides higher quality images of the pelvic floor and viscera. This sequence of MRI images shows a patient with ODS due to a high-grade recto-anal interception. MRI also allows accurate management of all three pelvic floor compartments.

However, whilst providing accurate images, MRI can both understage and also fail to diagnose rectal interception. We have found this looking at the data from our own unit. And thus we seek to complement cases of suspected IRP with an EUA. A clear circular anal dilator, such as the one shown here, is a very accurate way of demonstrating an internal rectal prolapse and also assessing its grade. we use the Oxford grading system. It overcomes a limitation of proctography and also allows anterior or posterior predominance to be noted. As well as for diagnosing internal rectal prolapse, crucially, we advocate the use of a CAD during corrective surgery to ensure that the IRP has been fully reduced prior to fixation of the rectum to ensure anatomical correction. This first video highlights the importance of using the circular anal dilator at the time of surgery to ensure that the rectum is adequately elevated out of the pelvis to reduce the internal rectal prolapse.

The surgical team mobilizes the sigmoid colon back to the embryological midline, followed by mobilization of the rectum down to the pelvic floor in the TME-HODY plane. After resecting the redundant sigmoid loop, a stapled colorectal anastomosis is performed, confirmed to be airtight and well-perfused via endoscopy.

Three months following the procedure, the patient reports improved stool frequency and her ODS symptoms have improved. She is very satisfied with the outcome of surgery but longer term data is awaited.

During laparoscopic surgery, the surgeon frees the sigmoid colon from the vaginal vault, revealing a deep and wide pelvis. The sigmoid colon is resected after low division of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The rectum is fully mobilized in the TME plane down to the pelvic floor. A stapled side-to-end anastomosis is performed, confirmed to be airtight and well-perfused on flexible sigmoidoscopy.

When reviewed at twelve months post-surgery, the patient was considerably better with no further incontinence episodes and also resolution of the ADF symptoms, delighted with her outcome. In conclusion, we believe that the management of all patients with internal rectal prolapse ought to always commence conservatively, using the salt-to-cooking mantra. In cases that fail conservative management, we advocate discussion in a pelvic floor MDT, And in those select cases deemed appropriate for surgery, we strongly recommend the use of a circular anal dilator to ensure complete anatomical reduction of the prolapse. Thank you for listening.
